content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Heather C. Mc Laughlin 
City Attorney's Office 
City Hall 
250 East L Street 
Benicia, CA 94510 
April 20, 2017 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-075 
Dear Ms. Mc Laughlin: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Benicia Mayor Elizabeth 
Patterson and Councilmembers Alan Schwartzman and Tom Campbell regarding the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that this advice does not 
analyze any other area oflaw, including Section I 090, the Public Contract Code, or common law 
conflicts of interest. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. Because your questions are general in nature and not limited to a specific pending 
decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 
QUESTIONS 
I. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the Mayor and two Councilmembers 
from participating in decisions to temporarily authorize downtown establishments to play music 
beyond the standards of the current noise ordinance? 
2. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the Mayor and two Councilmembers 
from participating in decisions to amend the Downtown Mixed Use Master Plan ("DMUMP") 
based on staff recommendations once the review of the noise issues in the downtown area is 
completed? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-17-075 
Page No. 2 
1. Decisions concerning the temporary relief from the noise ordinance standards would not 
foreseeably and materially affect the market values of either the Mayor's interests or those of the 
two Councilmembers. 
2. As explained below, the two Councilmembers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
any decisions concerning amendment to the DMUMP. However, such decisions will not 
foreseeably and materially affect the real property interest of the Mayor and she may therefore 
participate in those decisions. 
FACTS 
City staff has requested direction from the City Council because of complaints regarding 
noise from an establishment named Lucca's Beer Garden. Specifically, the February 7, 2017 staff 
report requested direction from the City Council to research issues related to the Downtown Mixed 
Use Master Plan ("DMUMP") regarding outdoor live music in the downtown area. 
Mayor Patterson and Councilmembers Schwartzman and Campbell all have real property 
interests in or close to the DMUMP area. Mayor Patterson owns a home that is located just a little 
over 500 feet from the boundaries of the DMUMP. Councilmember Schwarzman owns property 
within the DMUMP. He lives on and operates his business from the property. Councilmember 
Campbell owns property within the DMUMP from which he operates his business. 
At the City council meeting on February 7, 2017, Mayor Patterson, Councilmember 
Campbell and Councilmember Schwartzman drew straws to determine who could participate in the 
decision. Councilmember Schwartzman was selected to participate. The City Council directed staff 
to look at the noise issues. Staff has interpreted the direction as examining the noise issues at 
Lucca's Beer Gardens and the broader downtown area. 
Because staff will not finish its review of the noise issues prior to the start of the outdoor 
music season, it anticipates that either Lucca's Beer Garden or the Downtown Business Alliance 
will file "Petition for Temporary Relief from Regulations" to allow Lucca's and/or others similarly 
situated to play music beyond the standards of the noise ordinance. This type of action is authorized 
by Benicia Municipal Code Section 8.20.210: 
Any person may petition the city council for temporary relief from provisions of 
this chapter by written request directed to the city clerk. The city council shall 
hear such petition at the next regular city council meeting not less than five days 
after the filing of said request. Upon hearing all the evidence, the city council may 
grant temporary relief from the provisions of this chapter upon such terms and 
conditions as the council may see fit if the council finds that the temporary relief 
does not endanger the health, welfare or safety of the residents of the city. The 
temporary relief may not exceed 30 days in duration. The applicant for relief may 
not exceed the provisions of this chapter until such time as the permit of the city 
has been issued. The decision of the city council shall be final. 


File No. 1-17-075 
Page No. 3 
Once the review of the noise issues in the downtown area is completed, the City Council 
will review the recommendations of staff and the consultant and be requested to act. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
interests set forth in Section 87103 as follows: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103 ( e ). ) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.3 (Section 87103.) 
The specific interests relevant to all three officials are: 
I. Business Entities: The businesses owned by Councilmembers Schwartzman and 
Campbell assuming the value of their businesses is $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
2. Real Property: The residences of Mayor Patterson and Councilmember Schwartzman, and 
the office building of Councilmember Campbell. (Section 87103(b).) 
3 However, Regulation 18702.5 which provides that materiality standard for a disqualifying financial effect on 
an official's personal finances provides that if the governmental decision also affects a business entity or real property in 
which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and materiality is determined 
under Regulation 18702.1 (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not 
further analyze possible effects on personal finances. 


File No. 1-17-075 
Page No. 4 
3. Sources oflncome: The businesses operated by Councilmembers Schwartzman and 
Campbell assuming their pro rata share of income from the businesses is $500 or more in the 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)4 
All three officials would therefore be required to disqualify themselves from participating in 
decisions of the City Council which would foreseeably and materially affect their business or real 
property interests, or any sources of income to them, in a manner distinguishable from the effect on 
the public generally. 
Petitio11s for Temporary Relief from Reg11latio11s 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Business Interests: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest 
is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, 
a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. Where the financial interest is not explicitly 
involved in a decision, such as here, the foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can 
be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 
I8701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity. 
That regulation also provides various examples of the types of decisions that may impact the 
analysis, none of which is particularly relevant to the present situation. 
As mentioned, staff anticipates that, prior to finishing its review of the noise issues, either 
Lucca's Beer Garden or the Downtown Business Alliance will petition the City for authorization to 
play music beyond the standards of the noise ordinance. If the City Council grants the petition, 
however, it will provide only temporary relief from the noise standards not to exceed 30 days in 
duration. Nothing in your facts suggests such temporary relief from these standards, especially 
where there is no indication that Councilmembers Schwartzman and Campbell operate businesses 
that would even apply for or be impacted by such relief in the first place, would in any manner 
change the value of their businesses. 
Real Property: A financial effect on real property is material if it meets any of the standards 
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) through (12). The standards relevant to the decision in question are: 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
4 They would also have an interest in any clients of their businesses where their pro rata share of income 
derived from a particular client aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. 
(Section 87103(c).) We do not analyze potential conflicts of interest related to clients who may sources of income 
because you have not provided facts pertaining to any clients. 


File No. 1-17-075 
Page No. 5 
( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may 
provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
' 
If the City Council grants relief from the standards under the current noise ordinance to a 
particular establishment, it could have the effect of substantially altering noise levels at the real 
properties owned by one or more of the Councilmembers, depending on the location of the 
establishment. However, any impact from increased noise levels would be temporary, as explained 
above. In this regard, decisions concerning the temporary permits would not foreseeably and 
materially affect the market values of the Mayor's/Councilmembers' real properties.5 
Decisio11s Related to the DMUMP 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
As mentioned above, the standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest 
is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, 
a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a 
decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the 
official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701 (a).) An interest "is the subject of the proceeding 
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, 
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental 
decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2( a)( l ). " 
(Ibid.) 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) states: 
(a) Except as provided in subdivision (c) below, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision (listed below in (a){l) through (a)(l2)) 
on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than 
a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision: 
5 Subdivision (a)(l l) would only apply to the residential real property ofCouncilmember Schwarzman who 
owns residential real property within the boundaries of the DMUMP. We note that our conclusion would not change 
even if the pennitted establishment were within 500 feet of his residence as the temporary nature of the pennit in 
question would not affect the value of his residence. 


File No. 1-17-075 
Page No. 6 
(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as provided 
below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries 
of the plan. 
Your facts suggest that the City Council may consider amending the DMUMP based on 
staff recommendations once the review of the noise issues in the downtown area is completed. 
Participating in decisions that may lead to amendment of the DMUMP would be decisions that fall 
under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l ).6 
Accordingly, both Councilmember Schwartzman and Campbell have real property interests 
within DMUMP area that would be explicitly involved in all decisions related to amendment of the 
DMUMP, and the effect of the decisions would be both foreseeable and material. Therefore, they 
may not make, participate in making, or use their positions to influence these decisions. 
Mayor Patterson's real property interest is just beyond 500 feet from the boundaries of the 
DMUMP area. Her interest would therefore not be explicitly involved in future decisions 
concerning amendment to the DMUMP. Instead, whether the financial effect on her real property is 
material is determined by the standards in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l 0) and (a)(l 2), referenced above. 
You have provided no facts to suggest that amending the DMUMP to address noise issues in 
the downtown area would impact the real property interest of Mayor Patterson in a manner that 
would influence the market value of her residence. Accordingly, based on the general facts you 
have provided, Mayor Patterson may participate in these decisions. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
oo side 
ior Counsel, Legal Division 
6 You have not identified the "Downtown Mixed Use Master Plan" as a "specific" plan, but as stated by the 
Governor's Office of Planning and Research, "[p ]racticing planners in California have used a variety of euphemisms to 
describe specific plans. The creative use and combination of planning terms to describe various planning tools has 
blurred the distinction between specific plans, community plans, and area plans to the extent that the terms are often 
misused." (The Planner's Guide to Specific Plans (200 I) available at https://www.opr.ca.gov/docs/specific_plans.pdf.) 
To the extent that a "master" plan is indeed a "specific" plan or involves the amendment of the general plan, a decision 
involving the plan falls within Regulation 18702.2(a)(I). (See Houston Advice Letter, No. 1-16-095.)

completed?2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

1. Decisions concerning the temporary relief from the noise ordinance standards would not 
foreseeably and materially affect the market values of either the Mayor's interests or those of the 
two Councilmembers. 
2. As explained below, the two Councilmembers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
any decisions concerning amendment to the DMUMP. However, such decisions will not 
foreseeably and materially affect the real property interest of the Mayor and she may therefore 
participate in those decisions.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
interests set forth in Section 87103 as follows: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103 ( e ). ) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.3 (Section 87103.) 
The specific interests relevant to all three officials are: 
I. Business Entities: The businesses owned by Councilmembers Schwartzman and 
Campbell assuming the value of their businesses is $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
2. Real Property: The residences of Mayor Patterson and Councilmember Schwartzman, and 
the office building of Councilmember Campbell. (Section 87103(b).) 
3 However, Regulation 18702.5 which provides that materiality standard for a disqualifying financial effect on 
an official's personal finances provides that if the governmental decision also affects a business entity or real property in 
which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and materiality is determined 
under Regulation 18702.1 (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not 
further analyze possible effects on personal finances.  
3. Sources oflncome: The businesses operated by Councilmembers Schwartzman and 
Campbell assuming their pro rata share of income from the businesses is $500 or more in the 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)4 
All three officials would therefore be required to disqualify themselves from participating in 
decisions of the City Council which would foreseeably and materially affect their business or real 
property interests, or any sources of income to them, in a manner distinguishable from the effect on 
the public generally. 
Petitio11s for Temporary Relief from Reg11latio11s 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Business Interests: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest 
is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, 
a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. Where the financial interest is not explicitly 
involved in a decision, such as here, the foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can 
be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 
I8701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity. 
That regulation also provides various examples of the types of decisions that may impact the 
analysis, none of which is particularly relevant to the present situation. 
As mentioned, staff anticipates that, prior to finishing its review of the noise issues, either 
Lucca's Beer Garden or the Downtown Business Alliance will petition the City for authorization to 
play music beyond the standards of the noise ordinance. If the City Council grants the petition, 
however, it will provide only temporary relief from the noise standards not to exceed 30 days in 
duration. Nothing in your facts suggests such temporary relief from these standards, especially 
where there is no indication that Councilmembers Schwartzman and Campbell operate businesses 
that would even apply for or be impacted by such relief in the first place, would in any manner 
change the value of their businesses. 
Real Property: A financial effect on real property is material if it meets any of the standards 
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) through (12). The standards relevant to the decision in question are: 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
4 They would also have an interest in any clients of their businesses where their pro rata share of income 
derived from a particular client aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. 
(Section 87103(c).) We do not analyze potential conflicts of interest related to clients who may sources of income 
because you have not provided facts pertaining to any clients.  
( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may 
provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
' 
If the City Council grants relief from the standards under the current noise ordinance to a 
particular establishment, it could have the effect of substantially altering noise levels at the real 
properties owned by one or more of the Councilmembers, depending on the location of the 
establishment. However, any impact from increased noise levels would be temporary, as explained 
above. In this regard, decisions concerning the temporary permits would not foreseeably and 
materially affect the market values of the Mayor's/Councilmembers' real properties.5 
Decisio11s Related to the DMUMP 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
As mentioned above, the standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest 
is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, 
a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a 
decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the 
official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701 (a).) An interest "is the subject of the proceeding 
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, 
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental 
decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2( a)( l ). " 
(Ibid.) 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) states: 
(a) Except as provided in subdivision (c) below, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision (listed below in (a){l) through (a)(l2)) 
on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than 
a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision: 
5 Subdivision (a)(l l) would only apply to the residential real property ofCouncilmember Schwarzman who 
owns residential real property within the boundaries of the DMUMP. We note that our conclusion would not change 
even if the pennitted establishment were within 500 feet of his residence as the temporary nature of the pennit in 
question would not affect the value of his residence.  
(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as provided 
below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries 
of the plan. 
Your facts suggest that the City Council may consider amending the DMUMP based on 
staff recommendations once the review of the noise issues in the downtown area is completed. 
Participating in decisions that may lead to amendment of the DMUMP would be decisions that fall 
under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l ).6 
Accordingly, both Councilmember Schwartzman and Campbell have real property interests 
within DMUMP area that would be explicitly involved in all decisions related to amendment of the 
DMUMP, and the effect of the decisions would be both foreseeable and material. Therefore, they 
may not make, participate in making, or use their positions to influence these decisions. 
Mayor Patterson's real property interest is just beyond 500 feet from the boundaries of the 
DMUMP area. Her interest would therefore not be explicitly involved in future decisions 
concerning amendment to the DMUMP. Instead, whether the financial effect on her real property is 
material is determined by the standards in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l 0) and (a)(l 2), referenced above. 
You have provided no facts to suggest that amending the DMUMP to address noise issues in 
the downtown area would impact the real property interest of Mayor Patterson in a manner that 
would influence the market value of her residence. Accordingly, based on the general facts you 
have provided, Mayor Patterson may participate in these decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the Mayor and two Councilmembers 
from participating in decisions to temporarily authorize downtown establishments to play music 
beyond the standards of the current noise ordinance? 
2. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the Mayor and two Councilmembers 
from participating in decisions to amend the Downtown Mixed Use Master Plan ("DMUMP") 
based on staff recommendations once the review of the noise issues in the downtown area is 
completed?2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: 1. Decisions concerning the temporary relief from the noise ordinance standards would not 
foreseeably and materially affect the market values of either the Mayor's interests or those of the 
two Councilmembers. 
2. As explained below, the two Councilmembers have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
any decisions concerning amendment to the DMUMP. However, such decisions will not 
foreseeably and materially affect the real property interest of the Mayor and she may therefore 
participate in those decisions.
